era_context: Identical to -2290 and -2291: Old Kingdom Egypt in decline, Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin, Indus Valley Civilization in mature phase, Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Anatolia.

events.title: Naram-Sin's military campaigns in the north and east
events.region: Mesopotamia and surrounding regions, Akkadian Empire
events.description: Naram-Sin's inscriptions record campaigns against the Lullubi (Zagros mountains), the Martu (Amorites, western steppe), and other highland and peripheral peoples. These campaigns are presented as triumphs in royal inscriptions, but they likely represent efforts to maintain control over increasingly restive tributary regions. The military expenditure required for these campaigns strains the empire's resources.
events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's campaigns are well-attested in contemporary inscriptions. The specific dating to -2292 is conventional and depends on chronological systems.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: continued urban development
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues its characteristic pattern of urban development, craft specialization, and standardized weights and measures. No major changes are evident in the archaeological record for this specific year; the civilization maintains its stable, hierarchical structure.

sources.name: Archaeological stratigraphy at Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The mature phase of the Indus Valley Civilization is archaeologically well-attested. However, the archaeological record does not permit dating to individual years; -2292 is assigned by convention.

events.title: Egyptian Sixth Dynasty: continued administrative fragmentation
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty continues its gradual loss of central authority. Provincial governors accumulate power and resources. The royal court's ability to command resources for major projects diminishes. This process is gradual rather than catastrophic, but by the end of the Sixth Dynasty (c. 2184 BCE), the central state will have effectively dissolved.
events.certainty_note: The trend of administrative fragmentation is well-attested. The specific dating to -2292 is conventional.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The broader narrative of gradual decline in Egypt and strain in Akkadia is supported by evidence, though the pace and nature of these processes are debated.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -2292 is sparse and does not permit meaningful year-by-year differentiation from -2290 and -2291. The assignment of distinct events to each year is largely artificial, reflecting the limitations of ancient chronology and the coarseness of available sources. For this period, it is more honest to acknowledge that we can identify broad trends (Akkadian military expansion, Egyptian decentralization, Indus Valley stability) but not specific annual events.

graph_edges.note: Naram-Sin's campaigns in -2292 are part of the same broader military strategy as those in -2290
